summary: George Hotz announces that his new venture, the tiny corp, has raised $5.1M to commoditize the petaflop and democratize AI compute. The company's strategy is to build superior software (tinygrad) for existing AMD hardware rather than tape out new chips, arguing that previous AI chip startups failed because they neglected software despite having working silicon. Hotz contends that AMD's RDNA3 hardware is excellent but unusable due to terrible software, and the tiny corp will write a complete framework, runtime, and driver from scratch. The immediate goal is to get AMD on MLPerf, with the long-term vision of breaking NVIDIA's monopoly and ensuring AI compute is commoditized across 50 competing companies.
key_points: AI chip startups failed not because of hardware but because they never wrote decent software frameworks, while NVIDIA dominates through software superiority
AMD's $999 7900XTX offers the best FLOPS per dollar on the market but remains unusable due to buggy drivers and broken ROCm software
tinygrad's 12-operation IR avoids Turing completeness, enabling static analysis of compute and memory that CUDA throws away
The tiny corp business model is selling computers for more than they cost to make, starting with the tinybox
Hiring is done exclusively through high-quality pull requests to tinygrad, not job interviews
money_quotes: We sell computers for more than they cost to make; I've been thinking about this one for a while.
With the way AI is going, we risk large entities controlling the majority of the compute in the world.
I do not want 'I think there's a world market for maybe five computers.' to ever be the world we live in.
The current crop of AI chip companies failed. Many of them managed to tape out chips, some of those chips even worked. But not a single one wrote a decent framework to use those chips.
I think the only way to start an AI chip company is to start with the software.
I promise it's better than the chip you taped out! Why are you so arrogant that you think you can make a better chip?
This is life on easy mode, and I still doubt any of those AI startups could have done it.
I don't want to live in a world of closed AI running in a cloud you've never seen, I want everyone to have an AI that they own.
key_insight: The path to breaking NVIDIA's AI compute monopoly runs through superior open-source software for existing commodity hardware, not through taping out new chips.
